   Then, find the character again.

But in C++, division of integers is already floor for positive numbers.

So, code outline:

bool isSubsequence(string s1, string s2) {
   // standard subsequence check
}

int computeK(string s1, string s2) {
   int k = 1;
   int s1_ptr = 0;
   for (char c : s2) {
       // find c in s1 starting from s1_ptr
       size_t pos = s1.find(c, s1_ptr);
       if (pos != string::npos) {
           s1_ptr = pos + 1;
       } else {
           // not found, need to loop
           k++;
           s1_ptr = 0;
           pos = s1.find(c);
           if (pos == string::npos) {
               // s2 is not a subsequence
               return -1; // or throw
           }
           s1_ptr = pos + 1;
       }
   }
   return k;
}

Then, in the main function:

if (!isSubsequence(s1, s2)) return 0;

int max_m = min(n1 / k, n2);

return max_m;

So, the code outline is:

class Solution {
public:
    int getMaxRepetitions(string s1, int n1, string s2, int n2) {
        if (!isSubsequence(s1, s2)) {
            return 0;
        }
        int k = computeK(s1, s2);
        int max_m = min(n1 / k, n2);
        return max_m;
    }
    
private:
    bool isSubsequence(string s1, string s2) {
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while (i < s1.size() && j < s2.size()) {
            if (s1[i] == s2[j]) {
                j++;
            }
            i++;
        }
        return j == s2.size();
    }
    
    int computeK(string s1, string s2) {
        int k = 1;
        int s1_ptr = 0;
        for (char c : s2) {
            size_t pos = s1.find(c, s1_ptr);
            if (pos != string::npos) {
                s1_ptr = pos + 1;
            } else {
                k++;
                s1_ptr = 0;
                pos = s1.find(c);
                if (pos == string::npos) {
                    return -1; // should not happen as isSubsequence is checked
                }
                s1_ptr = pos + 1;
            }
        }
        return k;
    }
};

Now, considering the constraints, the code should handle cases where s1 and s2 are up to 100 characters, which is manageable.

Efficiency: The isSubsequence function is O(M*N), where M is s1 length and N is s2 length. computeK is also O(M*N) in the worst case. Since M and N are up to 100, this is acceptable.

Time complexity: O(len(s1)*len(s2)) for both functions, which is acceptable.

Space complexity: O(1), as no extra space is used beyond variables.

Energy implications: Since the algorithm is efficient and doesn't require constructing large strings, energy usage is low.

solution:
```cpp
#include <string>
using namespace std;

class Solution {
public:
    int getMaxRepetitions(string s1, int n1, string s2, int n2) {
        if (!isSubsequence(s1, s2)) {
            return 0;
        }
        int k = computeK(s1, s2);
        return min(n1 / k, n2);
    }
    
private:
    bool isSubsequence(string s1, string s2) {
        int i = 0, j = 0;
        while (i < s1.size() && j < s2.size()) {
            if (s1[i] == s2[j]) {
                j++;
            }
            i++;
        }
        return j == s2.size();
    }
    
    int computeK(string s1, string s2) {
        int k = 1;
        int s1_ptr = 0;
        for (char c : s2) {
            size_t pos = s1.find(c, s1_ptr);
            if (pos != string::npos) {
                s1_ptr = pos + 1;
            } else {
                k++;
                s1_ptr = 0;
                pos = s1.find(c);
                if (pos == string::npos) {
                    return -1; // This case should not occur as isSubsequence is checked
                }
                s1_ptr = pos + 1;
            }
        }
        return k;
    }
};
```

efficiency: HIGH
time complexity: O(len(s1) * len(s2))
space complexity: O(1)
energy implications: LOW
explanation: The solution efficiently checks if s2 is a subsequence of s1 and calculates the minimal number of s1 repetitions needed to form s2. It then determines the maximum m by considering both the available repetitions of s1 and the given n2. This approach ensures optimal performance without constructing large strings.
